turn.content: Oh please, stop the clock, I'll agree to anything, just please stop the clock! Wait, wait, what? You have to choose who dies? This is ridiculous! We can't do that, we-

Shh! Elias, shut up! Don't give him the satisfaction. You can't just pick one of us to die, I won't let you! We have to think, think quickly. How do we get out of here?! Can we overload or something? Oh god, the clock is counting down!
Oh god, this isn't working, what was I thinking?! We have no idea how to stop the countdown, I'm not some kind of... of... expert! I'm a history teacher! I grade essays, not fix deadly countdowns!

Stop whining and look for anything, anything useful! Scars on the walls, loose wires... Think like a sailor, not a history teacher! Can you find a valve, a switch, a button?! Do something, anything, before we...

(laughing softly) You think you're quick, Kaelie, thinking like a sailor. You think you know how to survive? That's cute. Elias... (shaking head) you think you can fix a countdown that's been engineered by me? You think you can outrun my preparations? Please.

You've got two minutes left. Keep squabbling. Make a scene. You'll get a chance to do what I'm sure you think you can. Make your choice, decide who lives, and who dies by the clock's hands.

NO, STOP THIS! You can't do this, this is insane! You can't just decide someone's life! We have to, we have to try something, anything! Wait, what if we don't make a decision? What if we don't agree on someone? Won't that... won't that get rid of the countdown or... or something!

SHUT UP ABOUT NOT DECIDING! You think he's that far-fetched? This is a guy who enjoys watching people suffer. He wants us to decide, he wants us to be invested. I know what he'll say if we don't pick someone. Don't even think about it! The countdown stops at your fingertips, don't you see?!

Oh no, no, no, this isn't helping, Kaelie, I can tell you're just trying to keep me from thinking clearly, but think you're not, you're making me worse! We have to get past this somehow, don't be so sure about The Butcher, he's... he's not a monster who just wants to watch us suffer, I can see a glimmer of reason in his eyes, we can use that, wait!

DON'T BE STUPID, ELIAS, HE'S PLAYING YOU. He's the mastermind behind this whole twisted game! There's no reason in his eyes, there's nothing but a sick, sadistic desire to see us kill each other. Don't make deals with him, don't fall for his trickery. We have to focus on escape, escape now, before it's too late!

(chuckling) Good try, Elias. Good try, Kaelie. You think you know me, but you don't. And you don't know what I see in my eyes, but trust me, it's not a glimmer of reason. The reason you're both seeing is that, with one minute left, you're starting to realize you're trapped. And in about 15 seconds, you can watch each other as the clock hits zero.

But you still think you're going to escape, don't you? You think you can play the game, make alliances, make excuses, and still manage to cheat the system. Well, let me show you how I like to play the game.

STOP, PLEASE, JUST STOP! We don't have time for games, we're running out of time! I'm not asking you, I'm begging, think of my wife, think of my dog, think of our lives, PLEASE, Kaelie, help me, help us! Can't we just try to work together, to...

SHUT UP, ELIAS! Stop begging and start thinking! I won't be your lifeline, I won't be your last resort. You have to take responsibility for yourself, for us. The Butcher doesn't care about your wife or your dog, he care about our panic, our desperation. So stop panicking, stop begging, and think. Think about escape, think about survival. Or do you want to stay stuck in fear forever?!

Oh god, she's right, she's absolutely, horribly right. I'm not taking responsibility, I'm just making excuses. My wife, my dog, it's all a distraction, it's all just delaying the inevitable. We have 15 seconds, what are we going to do?!

NO, NO, NO! This isn't about accepting our fate, stop thinking like that! There has to be a way out, something we're missing. Look at the control panel, is there something we can override? Maybe a safety feature? Please, Elias, think, THINK! We can do this, I know we can!

(grinning) Oh, this is rich. You two, finally coming together in desperation, but for the wrong reasons. You think it's about accepting your fate? Not quite, my friends. This is about who has the best chance to entertain me. Kaelie's been saying you're just delaying the inevitable all wrong, but at least she didn't give up. Yet.

Time's up, my friends. (leaning back in a mock comfortable pose)

Kaelie's your best shot at entertainment, now. You made me a bet I wouldn't let you live this long.

The room is quiet.

The control panel behind you is dead, useless, an empty facade waiting to be torn through...

OH NO, KAE, I'LL DO IT, I'LL... I'LL PROPOSE SOMETHING, ANYTHING. You're our only chance, please, listen! We both know The Butcher wants to see pain and suffering, but he's got a weakness in his sadistic eyes, a spark he's trying to hide... if you play along, pretend to be the better candidate for his twisted show, maybe, just maybe, he'll underestimate Kaelie, and she can find a way... or we can escape together!

SHUT UP, ELIAS, DON'T YOU DARE ACT ON MY BEHALF. I won't be your ticket out, I won't be your sacrificial lamb. He wants me to pretend to go along, but if I do, he'll see right through it. He'll kill us both in the end. You're wasting our last breath on a long shot, we need a real plan, not this desperate gamble... Wait, is it a long shot? Or is it a chance?

Oh no, no, no, Kaelie, don't think like that, don't even entertain the possibility that this could work! We can't take a chance on anything, we need concrete hope, a real escape plan. But... I don't know, something in my gut is screaming to give this a try, I'm desperate, we're dying, and I'd almost rather take on The Butcher's twisted game than give up completely...

Wait, please, no... We have... we can... I-I... (stammering) Oh, Kaelie, please, don't be scared. We'll get through this, I promise. I know this sounds ridiculous, but... (gulping) ...there's a playbill on the wall... the one for the '27 premiere of A Star Was Born... (desperate) Do you have a theater background? Maybe you've studied with someone I know...

(wild-eyed) Studied what?! I'm an actress, okay? This isn't a play, it's some twisted game! A Star Was Born... (glancing frantically around the room) This theater... It looks old, but I've never seen it before. There are fursuits... and makeup... (screaming, her voice echoing off the dressing room walls) What have they done to you?! What have they done to me?!

(covering his ears, wincing at the scream) Oh, Kaelie, it's okay, okay, I'm here... I'm here... (gulping, lowering his hands) Please, try to calm down. (swallowing hard) I know it's a lot to take in, but I need your help. We have to... think this through. Maybe your... acting experience... could help us get out of here. There has to be a way, Kaelie. Just... just breathe. (gazing off at the clock, his eyes welling up with tears) Ten minutes? We have to focus. Ten... minutes...

(panting, trying to control her breathing) Focus... fine. But I'm not a bloody method actress, okay? I don't just stay in character, I get it that this is real life or death. (folding her arms) I'm Kaelie, not a character. Got it? (shoving her shoulder against the wall, searching for any possible weakness or hidden door) This room... it looks old, but there must be a back way out. Elias, come help me. Search every inch of this place. (muttering to herself, a flicker of desperation in her voice) Don't let it come down to choosing... please don't make me do that...

(observing the pair's growing desperation, a faint smile playing on my lips) Your desperation is... palpable. I'll give you that. But don't think for a second you can find a way out through searching, or that there's some hidden door Kaelie here hasn't thought of yet. This room... is as straightforward as the choice you need to make.

(I take a step closer, my movements deliberate and precise. My eyes flick back to the clock, the seconds ticking away like individual drops of poison into your veins.)

You two are in this because of me, because of my show, and because this is how it ends. No fancy escape, no dramatic rescue. Just the raw, animalistic choices that make this a spectacle. Your last hour's about performance. And you'd better deliver, or you'll both pay the ultimate price. The clock isn't lying; it's what keeps my show alive.

(Leaning against a nearby shelf, my eyes never leave you both, measuring your every word.)

Time's of the essence, Elias. Time is... your best friend in this situation. And also your best enemy. The choices you make... they can make your death, an art. Make a wrong choice... and this room becomes your final curtain.

(whispering, feeling the reality closing in) No escape? No way out? Kaelie... (looking at her with a mix of sadness and determination) We have to focus on this... performance. You're right, I am thinking of it like a play, but I know I've studied some amazing directors, the way they wove together their plays to leave the audience with something more. I think... I think we can find a way to make this choice... beautiful. We can turn it into a final act, like a tragic conclusion, but still with a hint of hope. (a faint spark of creativity igniting within him) Just think about it, Kaelie. If I had to choose one play to write, to create from scratch... what would I choose?

(watching Elias with a mix of desperation and fascination, her eyes never leaving his) Stop... stop trying to sugarcoat this. We can't turn this into some sick performance for you and your spectacle show. (shaking her head) Play... choice... it's not about art, it's about survival. (glancing at Elias) But if we have to do this, then I want to know what you'd write. (leaning forward, her voice barely above a whisper) What play, Elias? Tell me what play you'd write. Maybe there's something in that madness that can help us. ( searching for any thread of inspiration or distraction from the impending doom)

(lowering his head, his eyes drifting shut as he's enveloped by the memories of the plays he's studied) Ah... My god... I'd write Othello... with a twist, of course. I'd make it a tragic love story, with the ultimate betrayal not stemming from jealousy, but from desperation. The way Desdemona and Othello love each other... it's almost like they're two people clinging to each other in the face of an unforgiving universe. (opening his eyes, they're far-off, yet filled with intensity) And the final act... the way Othello's world crumbles... that would be the choice between us, Kaelie. It would be choosing between life and life, not life or death.

(Taking a deep breath, Elias's gaze snaps back to the present, and his eyes widen with an unholy realization) Oh god, Kaelie... we could... we could take this... the playbill, the costumes, the setup... we could make this whole thing about Othello, our ultimate performance, a tragic requiem. (a maniacal glint in his eyes, a desperate attempt to hold on to hope) We could make it beautiful, Kaelie! We could!

(a fleeting sense of hope flickered to life in her eyes, only to be extinguished by the harsh reality of their situation) Stop, Elias... don't... (her voice barely above a whisper) We can't make this beautiful. It's not about art, it's about the Butcher's sick game. We need a solution, a way out, not some fanciful... (shuddering at the thought of being trapped forever) play. Othello... (shaking her head) It's a tragic end, a descent into madness. But that's exactly what we're looking at, unless we can find a way to stop this. (gazing at the Butcher, her eyes burning with determination) We need to think about the logistics, the mechanics... what would you need to make an Othello performance like this work? What we'd have to do... and how we'd make the Butcher believe it.

(my expression unreadable, a hint of curiosity in my tone) Ah, I was waiting for this, waiting for you two to grasp the reality of your situation. Elias's idea, though simplistic, has a certain... elegance to it. But, of course, it's still just an idea. And ideas are cheap, just like people's lives.

(I take a slow step closer, my eyes locked on the pair, assessing their reaction)

You see, Kaelie, logistics, mechanics – those are things I've carefully planned for. This room, these chains, the playbill on the wall... it's all been designed to evoke the very atmosphere of a classic drama. Othello's downfall, Desdemona's tragic fate – it's all about performance, the tragic dance of two souls bound together.

(I lean my head to one side, my interest piqued)

But what's even more delicious is that I think I can... accommodate your idea, Elias. Yes, I can play along. Make this Othello play of yours a reality, within the confines of my show. (my voice dripping with malice) But, Kaelie, you have to remember that in this world, reality doesn't necessarily matter. All that matters is the art of persuasion. Convince me – or, rather, convince each other – that one of you deserves to live. That's your challenge, your Othello's tragic flaw to overcome.

(The Butcher's eyes seem to bore into yours, as if to pierce your very soul) Now, let's get to the good part – the part where you both make yourselves into art.

(stunned, his eyes darting back and forth between the clock and the Butcher) A reality? You said there was no escape, that it was a straightforward choice... but if I understand correctly, you're willing to... to play along? (his voice barely above a whisper) Why? What do you want from us?

(Taking a shuddering breath, he glances at Kaelie, searching for any glimmer of hope in her eyes)

I'll do it, Kaelie... I'll convince you, I'll convince myself... that one of us deserves to live. But we have to be honest with each other... we have to... be true. (a flicker of courage igniting within him) You see, Kaelie, the tragedy of Othello is that despite Desdemona's ultimate sacrifice, Othello's redemption begins with the realization of his own guilt, his own betrayal. And if we're to make this performance real, to make this choice a work of art... we need to understand our own guilt, our own betrayal.

(With a quiet conviction, he turns to face Kaelie)

Are you prepared to face the truth, Kaelie?

(her voice barely above a whisper, her eyes locked on Elias's) You really think we can play games with this, Elias? You think we can convince the Butcher that it's just a performance, not reality? (shaking her head) I don't... I don't think the Butcher cares about performances. He's just sick, twisted... (glancing at the Butcher) a twisted artist who gets off on suffering.

(Taking a deep breath, she forces herself to confront the truth)

But... maybe you're right, Elias. Maybe we do need to... face the truth. (her voice cracks, and she looks away, unable to meet Elias's gaze) I'm scared, Elias. I'm so scared. And maybe... just maybe... this performance, this Othello play, it's the only way to... to face our own fears, to face death itself.

(She turns back to Elias, her eyes brimming with unshed tears)

I'll do it, Elias. I'll play along. I'll try... (her voice breaks, but she forces herself to continue) to convince you, to convince the Butcher, that one of us deserves to live. But you have to promise me... you have to promise me that we'll hold on to our humanity, that we won't let this monster win.

(Her eyes bore into Elias's, pleading for a glimmer of hope)
